data.title: Capital Markets: Shaping And Scaling The Future
data.description:
By Seema Shah, Chief Global Strategist

Seema Shah, Chief Global Strategist at Principal Asset Management, participated on a panel at this year’s Milken Institute Global Conference that tackled the transformation of global capital markets amid persistent inflation, geopolitical tensions, and tariff-driven uncertainty. The panelists openly addressed topics affecting the global economy, exploring issues ranging from the increasing appeal of Europe, along with the impact of tariffs, over-regulation, defense spending, and other factors that will influence the future trajectory of the global economy.

Q: From an investor’s perspective, what is your view of the global economy today?

Seema Shah: A lot of today’s challenges trace back to COVID. Even though we'd like to forget about it, the pandemic triggered significant shifts, like the transfer of capital from government to corporations and households. This resulted in a surge in government debt, which is influencing the Trump administration's actions. Three interconnected policies are at play: 1) tariffs are being used to generate revenue, which could help offset proposed tax cuts; 2) deregulation aims to spur growth, allowing the government to grow out of some of its debt issues; and 3) I’m convinced that concerns about government debt will persist, leading to some hair-brained ideas from governments over the next decade. So, market and policy volatility are probably here to stay.

Treasury Secretary Bessent’s mention of deregulation and tax cuts indicates that the Trump Administration is aware of potential issues and wants to act proactively. Although an economic slowdown is likely, we still don’t anticipate a recession as corporate and household balance sheets look strong, and the last decade shows that policymakers are eager to step in at the first sign of trouble. Because of this eagerness, the business cycle, as we knew it, is almost completely dead.

This is important for investors. If the business cycle is essentially dead, we probably won’t see the robust returns in risk assets that we had after the global financial crisis. Over the next decade, we should expect low returns coupled with high volatility. This reality may drive more interest in private markets and global diversification as investors seek ways to achieve the strong outcomes they’ve experienced during the last 10 years.

Q: Many investors are looking at Europe, suggesting that this could be the moment to create multinational giants or at least larger companies that might appeal to investors. Do you agree with that sentiment, and are you advising clients to rethink exposures outside of the U.S.?

Seema Shah: Last year, former ECB President Mario Draghi released the “Draghi Report,” which proposed four objectives: boosting innovation, particularly in high technology, adopting a new industrial strategy, combining tax, trade, and foreign policy, and reforming competition law to facilitate mergers of European corporations. The report was widely praised for its valuable insights. But, funny enough, it was immediately put on the back burner because it called for deregulation—a topic too often dismissed in Europe.

As a result, investors have come to see Europe as a “perennial disappointment.” It tends to perform well for a few months, but then it just goes nowhere. However, I’m noticing a shift in how people are thinking about Europe.

For the first time in many years, portfolio managers are starting to consider that something worthwhile might happen there. I was recently in Germany, and the vibe was different. It wasn’t about losing faith in the U.S., worrying about market dysfunction, or the dollar disappearing. Instead, many see opportunities that have been stifled by regulations. For the first time that I can remember, European governments are actually talking about deregulation. Earlier, I was in Madrid, and investors there were skeptical. They believed the real game changer would be if the market started opening up. And for the first time I can remember, we’ve finally started to see that in recent weeks.

Investors aren’t looking to shift away from the U.S. entirely. Still, if there is a marginal dollar to invest, they are considering the global opportunity set, and Europe is starting to come into focus. As a result, I’m trying to stay optimistic. This year will likely be tough, but looking ahead five years, Europe seems like a much more interesting prospect than it has in the past.

Q: We’ve heard a lot about shifting production centers, reshoring, new industrial policies, and increasing defense spending in Europe. What do you make of that as an opportunity?

Seema Shah: It’s interesting to see what’s happening in Europe with defense spending, for example. A lot of people talk about it, but the truth is that about 50% of military expenses come from outside Europe. So, the impact on the real economy is fairly small.

Now, the European Commission is saying Europe needs to start producing more military equipment at home. But let’s be real—it’s not something that can happen overnight or even in five years. We’re probably looking at a timeline of 10 to 15 years for things like advanced military assets and munitions.

So, while it’s great to think about new trends in defense spending and industrial policy, we also need to keep in mind that this is a long process. We shouldn’t just focus on the here and now; we should really consider what’s going to happen over the next 5, 10, or even 15 years. That’s why, when thinking about Europe as an investment story, it’s important to look beyond the immediate headlines. The gradual shift toward greater self-reliance in defense and industrial capacity may not move markets tomorrow, but it lays the groundwork for a more resilient and strategically independent Europe. Over time, that could become a compelling pillar in a stronger, more viable investment thesis for the region.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Veolia: Your 2025 Pick For Waste, Water, And Recycling
data.description:
Dear readers/followers,

I've covered Veolia Environment (OTCPK:VEOEY) (OTCPK:VEOEF) and invested in the company over the past few years. I view this French/international recycling and processing company as one of the more underappreciated and potentially undervalued plays in the entire sector. When the company troughed back at the end of last year, I loaded up with my usual 1-1.5% target for my portfolio, and since the upside following that entry valuation, my position has expanded to 1.7%. That makes me significantly invested in the company (anything above 1.3% is significant given my degree of diversification).

At the beginning of May, we had the company release Q1'25 results - those are the results we'll take a look at today.

However, I want to start out/begin with the following. Despite the significant recovery since the lows in late 2024 and early 2025, the valuation that Veolia is being "presented at" today is too low for what the company is, according to me, likely to be worth.

Why is that?

That's what I will clarify - using both the Q1'25, other valuation targets for the company from other analysts, and show you how I reach my fair value of over €35/share for the company, making the company attractive even now.

Let's get going.

Veolia - Upside From One of the More Defensive Sectors

Let's begin with some basics from the quarterly. The level of EBITDA and profit was in line with my own assumption for what the company would be able to achieve given its current growth rate. It's also in line with what the company, if you look at my previous two articles, the last of which can be found here, assumed to be its 2025E results.

Furthermore, and one of the biggest news for the quarter, is the company's agreement to buy 30% of the shares, or a company share, of the Caisse de Depot et placement du Quebec, which is a Water tech/solutions asset, at a price of above $1.75B.

Why is this an important fact, and why is it important for Veolia's future rate of growth and its realization of profit?

Veolia's activity in Suez requires, for the realization of significant synergies, to buy out CDPQ - and this 30% stake is a good beginning for this. Deal numbers are okay - not great, but not bad either. Veolia's purchase comes at an EV/EBITDA multiple of 11x, which, according to company calculations, will come in at synergies of €90M. At that price, I don't see a valuation upside, I expect the leverage in the company to increase by 0.2x for the year, and I see the eventual EPS accretion at around 2%, at 2026E, as the company expects, with a slight potential increase to ROCE.

What I want you to focus on, rather than this singular deal, however, is the company's sector leadership in water tech. The company is growing, and the water technologies sector is one of the main reasons I invested in Veolia in the first place. Anything "water" with this sort of leadership is a moat, going forward, seeing the importance of such technology for both populations and countries.

Remember, Veolia is an industrial play on the things which I view as important to get energy/water/environment efficiency. This includes the handling of hazardous waste; it's one of the few publicly traded companies with this geographical reach, but it also includes bioenergy/flexibility as an attractive addition. In fact, all three segments are more or less of similar size, so the company is very diversified and attractive. And looking at every single segment for the period of Q1'25, you will see that not a single one is down - and the latter, bioenergy, is actually up over 16% in terms of revenues.

The company has been on a journey of efficiency and finding savings for a number of years - this has been part of the deal rationale for a similar number of years when it comes to things like Suez and other things. The recent deal in Water tech, despite increasing its leverage, has also not endangered the company's leverage or debt in any way - it's still comfortably below its targeted 3x, and the company's yield, despite its recent valuation increase, is still above 4.6%. My own YoC is above 5%. This is not only a safe and attractive company, it's also a company that can be considered "high yield" for what it does, given that waste and especially water companies typically come in at yields below 2%.

Furthermore, Veolia has a very strong track record when it comes to value creation from assets - so the current trajectory is one that promises, if we look at previous results, excellent trends on a forward basis. Even though the top line only grew 1.5%, 3.9% organic excluding energy price changes, I retain a positive view on the company's current valuation. This is despite slowing waste volume growth, (but price increases for waste handling).

To date in 2025, it's been 8 years since Suez acquired GE Water and 2 years since Veolia acquired Suez (which is most of WTS), and I believe there to be significant value to realize in the water segment, both organically and organically. Water treatment accounts for over 40% of the company's turnover. It's not just municipal/public concessions in France, even though it started out that way - the company now has global contracts.

Let me be unequivocally clear on this - water distribution is an uncyclical, inflation-indexed segment/industry. It has a massive economic moat due not only to this, but due to the overall duration of these concessions. They can be viewed without a doubt as defensive, intangible assets - and this is 25% of the company's business. The company' waste management has a moat due to its postcollection service as well as the ever-growing amount of waste, requiring Veolia's segments, and the combination of other segments and the efficient global scale, and cost advantages over individual heating services make this a very attractive segment as well - in no small part due to the pass-through nature of the contracts, protecting Veolia margins.

The way I view Veolia is that as long as the company a) continues to acquire inorganically at attractive multiples, and b) does this without raising leverage red flags, and c) continues to trade at undervaluation, there is no reason for being negative on this company.

Its 2027E strategic plan is a growing dividend and EPS growth of 10% through both the aforementioned efficiencies as well as further investments. Q1'25 confirms this in a big way because it is, as I said, in line with what the company expects. In fact, the company went ahead and confirmed 2025E as if this latest quarterly result 3 weeks back.

Finding realistic investment risks for Veolia at this valuation is not easy, but there are some at the very least worth mentioning, even if I, personally, view them as far less relevant.

First, in a lower inflation environment, the company's retention of cost savings will decrease - so when that turns around, expect Veolia to have a harder time keeping up growth. Any overall economic slowdown in the core European geography will also weigh on company growth - and the outlook on this is currently mixed. As a last risk, though it's perhaps more of an economic fact, the company's main driver to realize growth is currently efficiencies brought about by scaling up, done through inorganic growth at what we can see being rather expensive multiples (in the way that they result in very little accretion). So for Veolia to grow, it needs to do so slowly, "digesting" every M&A it does to make sure that the leverage does not grow out of control.

But bullish arguments? Many of those - the main ones being the Suez and recent Q1'25 M&A, and especially Suez was actually not expensive. When I calculate 2025-2027E, I further find the company's growth estimates to be extremely conservative. I expect that the targets will not only be hit, but significantly beaten - which is why I have a far more positive outlook for the company and what may happen. But perhaps the biggest positive is that legacy Veolia was priced as a cyclical company. The company's cyclical activities are now reduced to a minimum. Every single thing it works with is actually counter-cyclical in most ways.

This makes for an extremely attractive company at a low valuation.

Let's see what we have here in terms of that valuation.

Veolia's Valuation Remains Attractive Even After Q1'25

Veolia's main ticker is VIE, in Paris. It trades at a 14x P/E and a 4.63% native yield with a credit rating of BBB, and a current market cap of over €22B. The company hits or beats targets more than 62% of the time, and most of its misses are related to COVID-19. For the longer timeframe, the company's average P/E has trended closer to 20-22x P/E.

Given the company's conservative operational mix and excellent fundamentals, I am supportive of a fair value that tends closer to the 20x P/E range, not the 15x P/E range, which has no support in historical multiples. This recent decline, which started in 2021-2022, is due to the company's M&A activities, as I view it, but looking at the long term, I see a high likelihood for a recovery to premium multiples. This is not only due to historical factors, but also higher forward margins, better efficiencies, and an average rate of EPS growth of above 8% per year.

However, even if you were to calculate the company's fair value at 15x P/E, which it has not averaged for any period in the last 20 years, you would at this point, with these assumptions, still get an almost 15% annualized RoR, or almost 44% in 2–3 years.

That is, as you can see, with an assumption of fair value of less than €40/share. At a PT of €36.5/share, my fair value assumption is of course far higher than €40/share.

To remind you, the fair value is where I believe the company could go, not what you should buy at - what you should buy at is the price that guarantees your hurdle to that fair value - and for me, my hurdle is 15% annualized.

My fair value assumption is a P/E of over 18-19x for 2025-2027 following concession renewal expenses based on a dividend yield of 4.5% and an EBITDA CAGR of 5.2% for 2024-2029 (above the company's estimates), and over 8% EPS growth (above estimates again). I expect an EBITDA increase of 6.2% in 2025 to over €7.2B, outside of the current guidance range, due to cost savings and more synergies from Suez. Integral for growth on the longer timeframe up to 2030 will be cost savings of about €150-€175M annualized, with EBITDA reaching €8B as early as mid-2026 on NTM, or 2027 at the latest. I expect the company's expenses/CapEx to be relatively unchanged and work from a 6% WACC.

These assumptions in turn point to a fair value of just north of €45/share, or 18-19x P/E for 2025-2026, and almost €50/share if we look at 2027. Forecasting to 2027, we see an annualized rate of return at only 18.5x of over 23% per year, or almost 75% total RoR.

The key argument here is that you can potentially make this return by investing not in some higher-risk cyclical, overleveraged company, or uncertain IT play, but instead in water, waste and biofuel/energy.

That's why I view Veolia as one of the most attractive plays in Europe at this time, combining safety and appeal.

Due to its relatively unique structure, the company doesn't really have peers. If pressed, I would say that American Water Works (AWK) can be relevantly compared - but the latter has less than half the yield, and is also traded at a far narrower fair value upside, leading me to say that Veolia is clearly better valued.

This is further confirmed by other analyst targets for Veolia. 18 analysts follow the company, with a low-end PT of €25.8 and a high-end range of €45.6, close to my own. The average PT comes to €35.3/share, close to my own of €36.5/share, and out of 18 analysts, 14 analysts come in at either "BUY" or "Outperform". (Source: Paywalled Link TIKR.com)

I therefore view these assumptions as at least similarly valued, and would characterize the expectations for Veolia as similar. I'm happy to invest more in Veolia here, even if the upside is lower - and as mentioned, would view this company as one of my best, safe European picks at this time.

For Veolia, the ADR is VEOEY. It's a 0.5x ADR, which puts the target for it at around $21.48/share compared to the native ticker, and it's €36.5/share.

Thesis

  • Veolia is a great business in several great fields, and this upside has materialized since the full results for 2024 and now the Q1'25 results. They have some excellent moats, a solid history, and good future prospects. The Suez M&A only strengthened the company's next few years, and whatever downsides exist and uncertainties are there, I believe they're outweighed by the upside potential in this stock, further enhanced by more savings and efficiencies.
  • There are downsides and risks, of course - but these, at this valuation, are relatively small. A company such as this, with these sorts of operations, at this sort of price, is precisely the sort of investment you may want to make - at least it's the sort of investment that I, personally, want to go "deeper" in.
  • This upside can even go up to 15-25% if we allow for a premium valuation or even higher, historically accurate EPS growth (which I am open to), valuing the company at 18-19x P/E. My official PT is €36.5/share for May 2025, and I'm at a "BUY".

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators, and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 4/5 "stars" here, which is enough for an attractive thesis for the company. Based on this, and a valuation target of €36.5/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Buy" rating as of March 2025.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Judges Check Trump's Trade Authority
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Trade court strikes down Trump's April tariffs. (00:20) Elon Musk (TSLA) exits DOGE after criticizing Trump's 'big, beautiful' tax bill. (01:13) Nvidia’s (NVDA) Huang: China’s AI sector rapidly advancing without U.S. tech. (02:11)

This is an abridged transcript.

A federal trade court has struck down the "reciprocal" tariffs that President Trump imposed on what he called "Liberation Day."

The ruling puts an immediate stop to the collection of a sweeping set of levies that the president imposed.

The three-judge panel on the U.S. Court of International Trade ruled that Trump exceeded presidential authority on the April 2 tariffs placed on more than 180 countries and territories.

"The Constitution assigns Congress the exclusive powers to 'lay and collect Taxes, Duties, Imposts and Excises,' and to 'regulate Commerce with foreign Nations,' " the Court wrote in granting summary judgment to a set of plaintiffs on the case.

The Trump administration will appeal the ruling, but considering that the suing plaintiffs sought no narrow relief, tariff collection will stop immediately, barring an emergency stay.

Elon Musk is stepping down from his role as a senior adviser to President Donald Trump.

In a post on his social media platform X, Musk thanked Trump for the opportunity to lead the Department of Government Efficiency, commonly referred to as DOGE.

“The @DOGE mission will only strengthen over time as it becomes a way of life throughout the government,” Musk wrote.

Since the start of Trump’s second term in January, Musk has headed the Department of Government Efficiency, where he was charged with significantly reducing the size of the federal government.

However, the Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) and SpaceX CEO's exit comes after he criticized Trump's "big, beautiful" budget bill - the legislative centerpiece of the president's agenda.

In an interview to be aired June 1 on “CBS Sunday Morning,” Musk said the “big, beautiful bill” will not help the nation’s finances. In a clip he goes on to say that the bill “undermines the work that the DOGE team is doing.”

Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) CEO Jensen Huang said Wednesday that Chinese AI companies are stepping in to fill the gap left by the exit of U.S. firms from the market, and their technologies are rapidly gaining strength.

“The Chinese competitors have evolved,” he said in an interview with Bloomberg Television.

Huawei Technologies Co., a Chinese tech company blacklisted by the US government, has become “quite formidable,” he said.

U.S. export restrictions to China have effectively shut Nvidia out of the world’s largest chip market, and the company now expects to forfeit around $8 billion in sales as a result in the second quarter.

However, Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) is up 5% in premarket action after the company reported fiscal first-quarter results and guidance that topped expectations, despite the impact of added export control curbs to China.

What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha:

U.S. pauses exports of airplane engine tech to China - report

Google starts direct online sales in India of Pixel smartphones

Tesla eyes June 12 as the launch date for its robotaxi service in Austin

Catalyst watch:

  • Ashland (ASH) will hold its Innovation Day for analysts and investors at the company’s Bridgewater, New Jersey facility. The event will showcase the enhanced capabilities and expanded applications of Ashland's scalable technology platforms.

Now let’s take a look at the markets ahead of the opening bell. Dow, S&P and Nasdaq futures are in the green. Crude oil is up 1.2% at $62/barrel. Bitcoin is up 0.6% at $108,000. Gold is down 0.2% at $3,279.

The FTSE 100 is flat and the DAX is up 0.5%.

The biggest movers for the day premarket: HP (NYSE:HPQ) -8% – Shares plunged after the company reported mixed FQ2 results and issued disappointing guidance, sparking investor concern.

On today’s economic calendar:

  • 8:30 am GDP

  • 8:30 am Richmond Federal Reserve Bank President Thomas Barkin will participate in a fireside chat before the Housing Partnership Network.

  • 10:00 am Pending Home Sales Index

  • 10:40 am Chicago Federal Reserve Bank President Austan Goolsbee will participate in a moderated question-and-answer session before the 2025 Mackinac Policy Conference hosted by the Detroit Regional Chamber.

  • 12:00 pm EIA Petroleum Status Report

  • 4:00 pm San Francisco Federal Reserve Bank President Mary Daly will participate in a fireside chat before the Oakland Rotary Club.

data.title: PDD Holdings: Sell-Off Creates Big Opportunity
data.description:
PDD Holdings (NASDAQ:PDD) reported weaker-than-expected earnings for the first quarter amid a massive trade spat between the U.S. and China that negatively impacted the company’s e-Commerce platform. While Pinduoduo obviously has exposure to this tariff conflict through ownership of Temu, recent signs in the trade war indicate that both countries are going to negotiate a new trade deal, which should help normalize relationships between the two largest economies in the world. I see Pinduoduo’s 14% post-earnings sell-off as a buying opportunity for contrarian investors and would expect the company's share price to recover fairly quickly.

Previous rating

I rated Pinduoduo a strong buy in April – Extraordinarily Cheap – specifically because of the e-Commerce company's Temu-related growth potential. Temu is a discount-focused and discovery-oriented e-Commerce platform in China that markets directly to foreigners, mainly in the U.S. and Europe, as its main customers. With the U.S. recently declaring new tariffs on China, e-Commerce platforms like Temu, Shein, or Alibaba (BABA)-owned AliExpress have experienced serious earnings headwinds. The U.S. and China, however, recently agreed to pause tariffs for 90 days in order to work on a new trade framework. If trade conflicts get resolved, I would expect a continual recovery in the share prices of Chinese e-Commerce companies.

Pinduoduo still offers massive value

Not surprisingly, Pinduoduo reported weaker-than-expected earnings and revenues for the first fiscal quarter given the recent escalation in terms of tariffs: PDD Holdings reported adjusted EPS of $1.59, missing the estimate by $1.05 per-share. Revenues were reported at $13.3B which caused the e-Commerce company to miss the consensus estimate by $1.05B.

With new U.S. tariffs getting imposed in the first-quarter, Chinese e-Commerce companies were expected to take a hit in their businesses and top lines. In the first-quarter, Pinduoduo’s e-Commerce business generated 95.7B Chinese Yuan ($13.2B) in revenue, showing 10% year-over-year growth and marking a 14 PP growth deceleration compared to the previous quarter.

Both Pinduoduo’s operating income and net income tumbled in the first quarter as well: its operating income crashed 38% year-over-year to 16.1B Chinese Yuan ($2.2B) while its bottom line plunged by almost half (47% Y/Y) to 14.7B Chinese Yuan ($2.0B). Despite the massive earnings drop related to the escalating trade war between the U.S. and China; however, investors should note that the e-Commerce company remained widely profitable in terms of both metrics.

Pinduoduo has the lowest annual revenue volume when compared against Alibaba and JD.com (JD), but the second-highest market cap at $146B, making the company a serious player in the Chinese e-Commerce market. Alibaba is the biggest e-Commerce company in China with a market cap of $285B, but the firm's portfolio does not only include e-Commerce operations: Alibaba also owns a sizable Cloud operation and invests in many other businesses as well, including its own logistics platform. Pinduoduo, despite its smaller size, however, had the second-fastest top-line growth rate in the March quarter at 10% compared to 16% for JD and 7% for Alibaba.

The market for Chinese e-Commerce is growing consistently and recently companies like Alibaba and Pinduoduo have made an effort to grow their e-Commerce transactions outside of China. Despite those efforts, Chinese companies will remain mainly reliant on the prospects of the domestic e-Commerce market as China represents, with a population of 1.4B people, one of the most attractive geographies for commerce-oriented trading platforms in the world. According to eMarketer, the Chinese e-Commerce market is going to grow to a size close to $4.0T by the end of FY 2027, implying an average annual growth of ~8%.

Likely outcome of trade negotiations

The main reason why shares of Pinduoduo slumped 14% after the e-Commerce reported a massive earnings drop related to increasing tariffs on Chinese product imports into the U.S. in recent months. In May 2025, however, both the U.S. and China agreed to lower previously announced tariffs to 10% for a period of 90 days while they work on a reset of their trade relationship.

In my opinion, both the U.S. and China have an interest in resolving their issues amicably, which should ultimately lead to a mutually beneficial trade deal for all parties involved. The announcement of a permanent resolution to the trade conflict – which could include a deal to limit America's trade deficit and ensure market access for American companies to China, especially in the automotive and financial industries – may ultimately be a very powerful, uncertainty-reducing catalyst for e-Commerce companies in general.

Pinduoduo’s valuation

Every time stock prices crash after an earnings report, I believe a buying opportunity is being created for long-term oriented growth investors, and this certainly seems to be the case here: shares of Pinduoduo are trading at a price-to-earnings ratio based on FY 2026 earnings of only 7.8X compared to a 3-year average price-to-earnings ratio of 9.4X.

Pinduoduo is not the only Chinese e-Commerce company that is trading at a bargain valuation, however. JD is equally cheap, trading at a forward P/E ratio of ~6.7X. JD is seeing solid top-line momentum and its shares are trading at a massive 15% earnings yield, which is why I called the e-Commerce company seriously mispriced in my last work in May.

Alibaba is slightly more expensive, but still a bargain from an earnings point of view: shares of the e-Commerce/Cloud company are trading at a forward price-to-earnings ratio of 10.7X. Alibaba's March quarter results were very solid given the backdrop in international trade, and the company saw some serious growth in its Cloud business, which is why I continued my strong buy rating: Alibaba Has Everything: Growth, Profits, And Value.

The industry group average price-to-earnings ratio is 8.4X, implying a 7% discount for Pinduoduo. However, the e-Commerce industry as a whole seems to be undervalued, given that U.S. investors have been cautious about Chinese large-cap companies in general. With Chinese company often using opaque ownership structures and not being required to have their accounts audited, U.S. investors have been very hesitant investing in Chinese companies like Pinduoduo, Alibaba or JD.

In my opinion, shares of Pinduoduo could revalue to a price-to-earnings ratio of ~11-12X in the long term. Based off of a consensus FY 2026 estimate of $12.92 per-share, PDD Holdings could therefore have a fair value in a range of $142 to $155 per-share, assuming that the trade conflict between the U.S. and China gets resolved in 2025 and e-Commerce companies in China have much less earnings uncertainty than today.

Risks with Pinduoduo

The biggest risk for PDD Holdings is a continual escalation in the trade war between the U.S. and China, which would obviously very negatively affect the company’s Temu-platform which is oriented towards foreign buyers. Still, the majority of revenues and earnings are generated in China's domestic market. However, if a trade deal fails to get negotiated and high tariffs become a permanent fixture, Chinese e-Commerce companies like Pinduoduo may be the ones that suffer most.

Closing thoughts

Despite the headwinds in the core business, Pinduoduo did overall still well in Q1'25 and maintained decent profitability. Pinduoduo has been negatively affected by the trade war between the U.S. and China in the first-quarter, but I consider current uncertainty to be only of temporary nature. It is likely that both countries, the U.S. and China, are highly reliant on each other for trade and will ultimately hammer out a trade compromise at the conference table. Shares of Pinduoduo are highly attractively valued with a P/E ratio of less than 8.0X, and I recommend investors to buy the drop here, especially since investors may be overreacting to the company's Q1 earnings report on Tuesday.

data.title: Twilio: Prepare For Takeoff With 80%+ Potential Upside
data.description:
Introduction and Investment Thesis

I last wrote about Twilio (NYSE:TWLO) in January, where I reiterated my "buy" rating for the fourth time, after turning bullish in July 2024. While the stock is down close to 14% since the time of previous publication, underperforming the S&P 500, it is up over 100% since my initial "buy" rating last year.

In my previous post, I had highlighted some of the key takeaways from its Investor Presentation that led me to believe that the upside for the stock is far from over. In the Investor Presentation, the management had outlined that it expected to see its revenue accelerate in FY25, followed by growth in double digits from FY26 onwards with a non-GAAP operating margin of 21-22% by FY27 and a cumulative $3B in free cash flow during this period of time.

The management believed that it would be able to achieve its above targets by driving an AI-led product innovation roadmap and doubling down on its go-to-market levers to capture market share in a growing TAM (total addressable market) that is estimated to reach $158B by 2028.

Twilio reported its Q1 FY25 earnings on May 1, where revenue and non-GAAP income from operations grew 12% and 34% YoY, respectively, beating estimates. During the quarter, its Communications business continued to see revenue and profit expansion, while its Segment business showed some meaningful sign of progress as it grew 1% YoY after several quarters of stagnation/decline.

This was led by ongoing advancements in its AI product roadmap with the release of Conversation Relay and a new voice intelligence feature called Generative Custom Operator in beta. Simultaneously, the management discussed that it saw "solid growth" in its go-to-market levers, particularly cross-sell and multi-product adoption, which represents a huge opportunity.

However, forward FY25 revenue guidance reflected caution, in my opinion, where revenue is now expected to grow 8% YoY. In the meantime, when we look at forward consensus estimates for FY26 and beyond, it is yet to catch up to management's expectations of double-digit growth.

The way I see it, the forward consensus estimates are conservative and should be easily achievable as the AI deployment phase intensifies and organizations spend a growing portion of their budgets towards personalization and trusted communications at scale.

Still, taking the consensus growth estimates into account for risk management purposes, I believe there is significant upside left. As a result, I will reiterate my "buy" rating with a price target of $216, which represents a potential upside of 85%.

Q1 FY25 Highlights: Third Consecutive Quarter of Revenue Acceleration, Segment Business Stabilizing and Growing Profitability

Twilio reported its Q1 FY25 earnings, where revenue grew 12% YoY to $1.172B, marking the third consecutive quarter of sequential acceleration.

Out of the $1.172B in revenue, the Communications business contributed over 93% of Total Revenue, growing at an accelerated pace of 13% YoY, driven by continued progress with Twilio's Messaging solution as well as its go-to-market growth levers that include ISVs, self-serve, cross-sell, and international expansion.

Meanwhile, its Segment business also saw a 1% revenue growth after several quarters of decline/stagnation, contributing the remaining 7% of Total Revenue.

In terms of profitability, Twilio generated $213M in non-GAAP operating income, which grew over 34% YoY, with a margin expansion of 300 basis points to 18.2%. This was driven entirely by the Communications business, which generated $277M in non-GAAP operating income, with a sequential margin expansion of 70 basis points to 25.3%. In the meantime, the Segment business continued to drag down profitability with a -2.0% non-GAAP operating margin.

However, I will point out that the management has shown tremendous progress in right-sizing their cost base and stabilizing growth in their Segment business, which was one of their strategic priorities in the last fiscal year. The management expects non-GAAP profitability to reach break-even for its Segment business next quarter while simultaneously seeing growing AE productivity and win rates, along with a meaningful reduction in churn and contraction in the quarter. This has led to its dollar-based net expansion rate to continue expanding for the third consecutive quarter in a row to 107%.

Tracking Progress Towards Its Long-Term Financial Goals From Investor Day

On that note, I will spend some time discussing how Twilio's Q1 results showcase progress towards goals that were set in its Investor Presentation at the beginning of this year.

As the management laid out, it was targeting a non-GAAP operating margin of 21-22% between 2025 and 2027, while generating a cumulative $3B in free cash flow during this period of time. At the same time, it expected to achieve GAAP profitability by Q4 FY24 and remain that way for FY25 and beyond.

What was also important to note was that the company expected to grow in double digits in revenue growth from FY26 onwards, and even if that doesn't happen for unforeseen reasons, they would still manage to deliver a 21-22% non-GAAP operating margin between 2025 and 2027.

But first, in order to reach the above financial targets, the management set its expectation on its TAM to total $158B by 2028, which will consist of its Communications and Data (Segment) market growing at an 11% compounded annual growth rate to $119B, along with an emerging market, CXaaS (Customer Experience as a Service), that should add another $39B, driven by Conversational AI and Orchestration.

Second, Twilio would need a targeted product roadmap to capture a growing portion of the TAM in order to reach its above financial targets by 2028. During their Investor Presentation, the management shared the following slide that provides a summary of its AI products and features across Communications, Data (Segment) and emerging CXaaS (Comms+Data+AI) businesses, where products such as Verify Fraud Guard, Unified Profiles & Agent Copilot, AI-Powered Predictions, and Predictive Audience are driving greater customer value and higher ROI.

During Q1, Twilio continued to advance its AI-powered innovation roadmap with the launch of the Conversation Relay product, which is proving to be a great tool for helping developers easily build AI voice agents, according to management. They recently entered into a partnership with Eleven Labs, which will further bring over 1000 natural-sounding voices in over 40 languages to Twilio's Conversation Relay, thus delivering a low-latency and high-fidelity conversational experience for their customers.

Plus, they also released a new voice intelligence feature called Generative Custom Operators in public beta, which helps customers automate complex tasks across use cases spanning call scoring, conversation topic detection, compliance monitoring, and more.

In all of this, it is important to understand that companies are still early in their AI deployment plans, which I believe gives Twilio a vast opportunity to drive deeper adoption of its Communications products, along with driving better synergies with its Segment business, as organizations deploy a higher share of their budget towards driving personalized and trusted communications with their customers at scale.

Third, Twilio needs to double down on its go-to-market strategy, which consists of self-serve, cross-serve, ISVs, and international markets.

During the Investor Presentation, the management shared that its Self Serve cohort accounted for 15% of Total Revenue, while Cross-Sell and ISV accounted for 7% and 24%, respectively. Meanwhile, International markets generated 35% of Total Revenue and continue to be an underpenetrated market.

However, it is the "cross-sell" opportunity that represented the largest growth lever in my opinion, where 63% of Twilio customers purchased a single product, while 90% of their revenue was generated by multi-product customers.

During the quarter, the management said that they saw "solid growth" in cross-sell and multi-product adoption with Voice Intelligence, Verify, SMS Pumping Protection, and more. What was particularly interesting in management commentary was that customers who were coming inbound tended to have a higher demand and interest in consuming more of Twilio products across channels, which made it easy for AEs. At the same time, the management also emphasized that they are putting a lot of investment into integrating technologies deeply together from an R&D perspective to make the customer experience more frictionless.

FY25 Guidance Reflects Caution - Consensus Estimates Don't See a Return to Double-Digit Growth

Looking forward, the management slightly raised their forward revenue guidance from their previous estimates by roughly 50 basis points to a year-over-year growth rate of 8%. Meanwhile, forward consensus estimates are projecting FY25 revenue growth of 8.45% YoY to $4.83B. While it marks an acceleration on a year-over-year basis, the management continues to be prudent in the current volatile macroeconomic environment.

In the meantime, the management raised their projection for non-GAAP income from operations by close to 3% to $862.5M, which would represent a margin of close to 18%, up 200 basis points from a year ago.

However, what is slightly concerning is that despite management expecting revenue growth to return to the double digits from FY26 onwards, consensus estimates haven't caught up yet, as can be seen below.

On one hand, it represents that investors are not confident that the management will be able to execute per guidance. On the contrary, if the management proves itself to be capable of returning the company to a double-digit growth from FY26 onwards, we could expect a sizable rerating from the current environment.

Revisiting My Valuation

Looking forward, I will take consensus estimates for revenue through FY25 to FY27, where the company is expected to grow in the high single digits to $5.65B by FY27. Note that this is below what the management expects, where it believes that it should be able to return the company to growing in the double-digit range. The way I see it, as the AI deployment phase intensifies, organizations should be willing to spend a greater percentage of their budgets on personalization and trusted communications at scale with their customers, which should translate into significant upside for Twilio's suite of products, thus making me believe that the current consensus estimates should be easily achievable.

I believe that the consensus revenue growth estimates will be easily achievable, especially as businesses

From a profitability standpoint, I will take management's guidance for non-GAAP operating income for FY25 and expect it will grow to 21.5% by FY27 as per their long-term financial goals, as they are able to drive deeper adoption of their solutions suite across its Communications and Segment businesses, leading to higher spend per customer.

This will translate to a total non-GAAP operating income of $1.216B by FY27, which will be equivalent to a present value of $965M when discounted at 8%.

With earnings projected to grow at an 18% compounded annual growth rate during this period of time, I believe it deserves a 2x the average S&P 500 price-to-earnings multiple, where its companies grow at an average rate of 8% every year.

This will translate to a price-to-earnings multiple of 34, or a price target of $216, which represents an upside of 85% from its current levels.

My Final Verdict and Conclusions

Under the current conservative growth estimates, I believe that the stock is undervalued. Let's not ignore the fact that should management prove themselves to be capable of generating double-digit revenue growth from FY26 onwards, we will witness a significant rerating of forward estimates.

data.title: TCW Relative Value Large Cap Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Investment Environment

Stocks, as represented by the broad-based S&P 500, ended the quarter in the negative with most of the decline occurring in March amid tariff threats combined with heightened concerns of a slowing economy, diminishing consumer confidence, and fears that inflation may reverse course after making much progress toward the Federal Reserve’s (the Fed) 2% goal. Though slower than the prior quarter, the U.S. economy grew by 2.4% in the fourth quarter of 2024. A strong double-digit 4Q:24 earnings season took a backseat to new regime uncertainties. Financials were the best performers followed by Tech+. There was dispersion among the Magnificent 7. Meta† reported beats on both the bottom and top lines while Google’s† (GOOG,GOOGL) stock sold off sharply over concerns that the company’s weaker-than-expected cloud growth and big capex investment. Amazon† (AMZN) fell after stating revenue was constrained in its Amazon Web Services division and guidance was less than expected. Nvidia† (NVDA) reported results that topped analysts’ estimates but gave a weaker than expected quarterly forecast for gross margins.

The February jobs report was roughly in line with estimates, and while the unemployment rate ticked up, it remains near historical lows. Average hourly earnings were up 4.0% y/y (as of February) still providing consumers income above inflation. The ISM PMI read for February was a tad over 50 (denoting expansion) for the second consecutive month. However, the new orders and employment components were both back in contraction. While job growth remains solid, and the consumer is in relatively good shape, weak January and February macroeconomic releases stoked concerns the U.S. economy has entered a slow-growth patch which has been exacerbated by recent tariff announcements. Atlanta Fed’s GDPNow estimate model saw a sharp revision to its 1Q:25 GDP forecast from the 3%+ level early in the year to now flat to negative.

As expected, the Fed left the fed funds rate unchanged in March but slowed the pace of its Treasury balance sheet runoff to $5 billion/month from $25 billion to begin April 1 (mortgages are continuing the runoff at the current pace) and kept its forecast for two cuts later this year. It also lowered the U.S. GDP forecast to 1.7% for 2025 and raised inflation to 2.8%. In his comments, Chair Powell said, “We do not need to be in a hurry to adjust our policy stance, and we are well-positioned to wait for greater clarity.” He added that the (U.S.) “economy is strong overall,” “labor market conditions are solid,” and wage growth is outpacing inflation.

Sector Weightings 1

Over the course of the quarter, there was wide dispersion among the eleven major economic sectors. At the top end, the best performers in the Russell 1000 Value were energy (+9.5%), communication services (+7.0%), health care (+6.4%), and utilities (+6.2%) while at the bottom, and in the negative, the laggards were information technology (-5.7%), consumer discretionary (-4.6%), and industrials (-2.9%). The portfolio sector’s weightings were a negative detracting over 70 bps due largely to the overweights in IT and consumer discretionary, two sectors which are perhaps most vulnerable to tariffs, and underweights in the more defensive and yield-oriented health care and consumer staples groups given the decline in interest rates. The underweight in industrials was a positive.

Security Selection* 1

The underperformance occurred in March as stocks became highly correlated with tariff fears with few distinctions. Usually a positive, stock selection detracted. The portfolio’s health care names were the best relative performers returning 13.2% besting the group’s 6.4% gain led by McKesson (MCK; 3.95%**) and biopharmaceuticals Amgen (AMGN; 2.19%**) and AbbVie (ABBV; 2.81%**). GE Aerospace (GE; 3.07%**) shone in industrials where the portfolio’s holdings gained 1.1% versus a -2.9% drop for their peers. McKesson’s stock rose nearly 20% in the quarter after reporting solid EPS and revenue growth driven by increased prescription and specialty product volumes. GE gained 20% as the company’s strong aftermarket business from legacy commercial aircraft engines outperformed analyst expectations for earnings and free cash flow. Despite the broad sell- off in IT stocks, IBM (IBM; 4.61%**) bucked the trend, rising 14% in the quarter due to increased conviction in the company’s turnaround with its emergence in the cloud and AI markets. Intercontinental Exchange (ICE; 3.75%**) rose as it benefited from greater trading activity associated with increased volatility.

On the downside, the portfolio’s financials names lost -1.1% behind group gain of 2.7% due largely to alternative asset managers Apollo Global Management (APO; 1.87%**) and KKR (KKR; 1.52%**) whose stocks fell in concert with their peers as global growth/recessionary concerns slowed deployment and monetization activity. However, during recent meetings, managements have stated in slowdown scenario, they have ample dry powder to take advantage of forced selling opportunities. IT stocks sold off in the wake of the Deep Seek disruption starting in January and more recently as a result of trade barriers walking off willing buyers. The hardest hit portfolio names were AI, hardware, and software company Broadcom† (AVGO), contract manufacturer Flex (FLEX; 1.92%**), and data storage company Netapp (NTAP; 1.48%**). Broadcom’s stock had returned 110% in 2024 with several sales last year as it breeched greater than 5% at market and then again this year as it appreciated to the point of no longer meeting any of the five valuation factors. It was completely sold during the quarter.

Representative Buys (Ticker, Sector)* Equitable Holdings (EQH; Financials; 0.46%**) MSCI (MSCI) ESG Score 2: Overall AA

Equitable Holdings, headquartered in New York, NY, is a financial services company primarily focused on life insurance segmented by protection services, individual and group retirement, as well as a fledging wealth management business and 62% ownership interest in asset manager, AllianceBernstein† (AB). At initiation, the stock had a market capitalization of $16.9 billion and met four of the five valuation factors, lower than market price-to-sales, price-to-earnings, and price-to-book and higher than market dividend yield. The primary catalyst is restructuring/cost saving following its second risk transfer deal (February 2025) with Reinsurance Group of America†. The deal offloaded 75% of its individual life block worth $32 billion, creating $2 billion of value, with which Equitable will repurchase $500 million worth of shares and launch a $1.8 billion tender offer for AB which could potentially bring up its ownership to 77.5%. This follows a $12 billion variable annuity (VA) and $14 billion separate account assets reinsurance deal with Venerable in 2021. Both deals deemphasize capital intensive, market sensitive, low growth products, which command low multiples, in lieu of capital light, higher growth businesses such as asset (AM) and wealth management (WM) that carry higher multiples. Pro-forma for the most recent deal, operating earnings from AM and WM is expected to grow from approximately 33% to 39. The secondary catalyst is new markets/new products as Equitable has been an innovator introducing its buffered annuity product, Structured Capital Strategies (SCS), in 2010. More recently in May 2020, it introduced an updated version of SCS called the Dual Direction which provides limited upside potential when the market is up or down. Equitable is also involved with In-Plan Guarantees which allow participants to convert a portion of their retirement savings into a guaranteed income stream. In 2024, BlackRock† (BLK) partnered with Equitable to offer LifePath Paycheck to its clients, initially covering 500,000 employees (of a total of 35 million) in 14 plans with $27 billion in target date assets, while JPMorgan Asset Management (JPM; 4.14%**) will offer the SmartRetirement Lifetime Income to its clients, who collectively own $148 billion in target date fund assets. Equitable has divested some of its most capital intensive assets and is focusing on less capital intensive products without living benefits or interest rate risk. While SCS and In-Plan Guarantees will drive growth in life insurance, the mix will still skew toward AM and WM over time, and its multiples will accordingly follow.

Intel Corp. (INTC; Information Technology; 0.99%**) MSCI ESG Score: Overall AAA

Intel, headquartered in Santa Clara, CA, is the one of the leading manufacturers of microprocessors for the global computing market. At initiation, the stock had a market capitalization of $109 billion and met four of the five valuation factors (price-to- cash flow, price-to-book, price-to-sales, and dividend yield). The fundamental catalysts are new management, new products, and restructuring. In an effort to turn around the company after several years of both disappointing strategy and poor execution, the company installed a new CEO Lip-Bu Tan who assumed the role on March 18, 2025. An accomplished technology leader with deep semiconductor industry experience, Mr. Tan was the CEO of Cadence Design Systems† (CDNS) from 2009 to 2021, a tenure during which he delivered strong earnings performance and increased shareholder value by more than doubling revenue and expanding margins resulting in strong improvement in ROE from negative in 2008 to 27% in 2021. Even though the full details of the new CEO strategy are not in place yet, we expect his top priority will be on offsetting losses at Intel foundry operations either through restructuring, partnerships, or outright sale. At the same time, we expect Intel to continue to solidify its client PC leadership and to emphasize its dedication to expanding its foothold in the AI-powered PC market with the planned introduction of the Panther Lake AI PC processor later this year or early 2026 in addition to products for data centers utilizing Xeon 6 technology. In September 2024, the company introduced its new Core Ultra 200-series processors under the code name Lunar Lake which are designed to emphasize low power/longer battery life and improvements to gaming. We believe the addition of Mr. Tan, a leading figure in the industry, resolution for the foundry loses, and the drive to focus on new products both at market and soon to enter should increase cash flow, operating margins, and earnings thus leading to a turnaround in Intel’s stock performance.

Texas Instruments (TXN: Information Technology; 1.72%**) MSCI ESG Score: Overall AAA

Texas Instruments, founded in 1930 and headquartered in Dallas, TX, engages in the design and manufacturing of semiconductors and a large player in the industry. Its products are utilized in the industrial, automotive, personal electronics, enterprise, and communication markets. The company operates through the following segments: Analog, Embedded Processing, and Other. The Analog segment semiconductors change real-world signals such as sound, temperature, pressure, and images by conditioning, amplifying, and often converting them to a stream of digital data that can be processed by other semiconductors such as embedded processors. The Embedded Processing segment involves handling specific tasks which can be optimized for various combinations of performance, power, and cost, depending on the application. At initiation TXN had a $176 billion market capitalization and met one valuation factor, dividend yield of 2.84%. The fundamental catalysts are new management and new markets/new products. Haviv Ilan assumed the CEO role in 2023 and he has embarked on modernizing and expanding the company’s capacity with a goal of manufacturing leading edge analog/mixed signal performance at significantly lower cost. TXN is also committed to expanding its broad line of over 80,000 products to meet new applications across current and emerging markets. TXN’s new leadership and product line expansions should help spur cash flow, operating margins, earnings, and dividend growth.

Representative Sells (Ticker Sector)*† Broadcom, Inc. (AVGO; Information Technology) MSCI ESG Score: Overall A

Broadcom, headquartered in San Jose, CA, is a leading provider of semiconductor devices for the communications and networking industries with a growing business in the enterprise security and software markets. The primary catalysts were new/strong management, new products/new markets, and restructuring. CEO, Hock Tan, has embarked on a series of acquisitions to enter new markets and grow free cash flow while expertly integrating the acquisitions to drive significant synergies. The company’s two most recent deals open new software markets of enterprise security and systems management.

These diversifying acquisitions carry high margin cash flows and recurring revenue that add stability to Broadcom’s financial performance and Broadcom is delivering. More importantly, the company’s customized product strategy in its semiconductor operation has opened new markets in the evolving AI market. Broadcom has generated over $12 billion in revenue from AI during 2024 and it is on track to deliver significantly more over the next few years. This successful strategy implementation is expected to result in a “cash flow machine” capable of producing significant growth in cash flow, margins, and earnings under the stewardship of the strong management team. However, the stock’s strong performance propelled it to no longer meeting any of the five valuation factors which warranted a complete sale.

Novartis AG Sponsored ADR (NVS; Healthcare) MSCI ESG Score: Overall AA

Novartis is headquartered in Basel, Switzerland, but maintains a significant presence in the U.S. The company develops, manufactures, and markets pharmaceutical products globally, including sizable business in both branded and generic drugs. At initiation, the stock met three of the five valuation factors (price-to-book, price-to-earnings, and dividend yield). The primary catalysts were new markets/new products and new management. Novartis is enjoying a period of strong new product launches, including new medicines for cancer, anti-inflammatory and cardiovascular disease, and ophthalmology. These launches and the company’s robust pipeline of late-stage development assets are supported by industry-leading R&D spending. The company’s CEO, with a background as Novartis’ Chief Medical Officer, took a strong path on its R&D-driven innovation strategy and divesture of non-core business to sharpen the company’s focus. Despite positive management execution of the company overall’s strategy, significant macro uncertainty, driven by U.S. unprecedent tariffs, we decided to reallocate resources to other investments.

nVent Electric (NVT; Industrials) MSCI ESG Score: Overall A

nVent Electric, headquartered in London for tax purposes and with operational headquarters in Minneapolis, MN, is an electrical products company. nVent’s products have a degree of pricing power for 1) they are a very low proportion of a project bill of materials and 2) the company’s products are designed for ease of installation which saves on expensive electrician labor costs and installation time. The company’s strong management team deserves credit for having aligned the company’s go-to market strategy, made tactical acquisitions in the data center electric products space, expanded its manufacturing capacity in its data center liquid cooling systems, and divested its non-core RayChem and NuHeat commercial and residential electric heat tracing systems. nVent is experiencing softer-than-expected sell-in to channel partners as distributors who pre-bought electrical components in anticipation of potential tariffs. The company has material exposure to both Canada and Mexico from both manufacturing and materials sourcing and which have the potential to impact should this risk materialize. Additionally, increased competition from other electrical products companies seeking to power and cool data centers are reasonably likely to pressure margin expansion in that end market for nVent. As such, for these primary reasons, the position was eliminated.

Market Outlook

We came into the year with expectations that the U.S. economy would continue to grow at around a 2% growth rate and that corporate profits (earnings per share) would advance this year at a rate in excess of 10%. EPS forecasts have been reduced to the high single digits and, in some cases, to the low single digits (3-4% per Goldman Sachs (GS), 3/31/25) due to the tariff economic drags. Surveys of CEOs and CFOs show that confidence has declined.

“Liberation Day” arrived after the close Wednesday, April 2nd and stocks globally sunk on back-and-forth retaliations with the biggest adversary and our largest importer, China. The onset of the trade war overshadowed the pretty good March jobs data. Even though the unemployment rate ticked up a notch to 4.2%, it is still near historical lows. Average hourly earnings y/y are up 3.8%, down from the prior month read of 4.0% but still outpacing inflation. While backward looking macroeconomic data suggests a still resilient U.S. economy, most indicators point to a significant slowdown. U.S. stock markets are one of the most important leading economic indicators. It is typical for the broad market to be down 3-5% four or five times a year, but uncertainty is increasing with severe potential economic implications.

Global manufacturing remains sluggish. While China’s Manufacturing PMI improved 0.4 ppts to 51.2, the highest since November, most other countries are in contraction territory. Indeed, global manufacturing PMIs show that one just one-third of the major PMIs are in expansion territory. This is the lowest level since September 2024. The weak level is notable. The last time we saw a trade war, manufacturing started from a much stronger base with much stronger momentum than it is now. There is less of an economic buffer this time around.

We are maintaining our “steady hand” with overall risk controls such as position sizes and sector guidelines and keeping eyes on the company specific catalysts that we believe will work in most macro environments. Our view is that it would be just like President Trump to start with big announcements but leave the door open for “negotiations.” April could mark the lows, and seasonally, second quarters are the next best performance months other than Q4. Europe has the potential to keep outperforming the U.S., but there are a lot of geopolitical risks to be considered there as well. Value should continue to outperform the broad U.S. market for the rest of the year, but it is never a straight line.

If current events lead to a liquidity crisis, there is no doubt the U.S. Federal Reserve board will step in although it would be better for everyone if it doesn’t come to that. A Reuters/Ipsos poll published on April 8 found that a majority (57%) of American adults oppose President Trump’s new tariffs so perhaps cooler heads will prevail. The Relative Value team reviewed tariff implications starting last February when it became clear President Trump was to be the Republican presidential nominee. While the breadth and the magnitude of the current tariffs are greater than the macro scenario testing, the portfolio was scrubbed last year, and the direction taken was correct. In addition, all holdings are continuously tested to meet minimum balance sheet and liquidity requirements in the event of a recession which would be the outcome of tariff demand destruction. It is mitigated by most companies shifting to local production for local demand following Trump 1.0 and revamping supply chains post pandemic. Technology and consumer discretionary goods are likely to be the most negatively affected but offer the most upside if negotiations and long-term policies are put in place. More domestic providers of healthcare, utilities, and food products should be the least impacted. The Relative Value diversification strategy of representing all 11 major economic sectors should prove beneficial over time.

We are busy and actively working to make the best portfolio decisions possible with the information available and will adjust should the “facts” change. We are constructive about the future but maintain strong risk controls for turbulent events such as these.

(PMI) U.S. ISM Manufacturing Purchasing Managers Index – An indicator of the economic health of the manufacturing sector. Price-to-Earnings Ratio (P/E)– A valuation ratio of a company’s current share price compared to its per-share earnings. Private Equity– An asset class consisting of equity securities and debt in operating companies that are not publicly traded on a stock exchange. Restructuring– A significant modification made to the debt, operations or structure of a company. Standard & Poor’s 500 Index (S&P 500)– An index of 505 stocks issued by 500 large companies with market capitalizations of at least $6.1 billion. It is seen as a leading indicator of U.S. equities and a reflection of the performance of the large-cap universe. Tariff– A tax imposed on imported goods and services. Underperform– When an investment is underperforming, it is not keeping pace with other securities or indices. Underweight– A condition where a portfolio does not hold a sufficient amount of a particular security when compared to the security’s weight in the underlying benchmark portfolio. data.title: Renewable Energy And Insurers: Tailor Made?
data.description:
By Xiaoyu Gu | Deanna Leighton, CFA | Gerry Anderson

Investment in clean energy infrastructure may tick a lot of boxes for life insurers.

Matching assets to long-term liabilities without compromising on return potential can be a challenge for insurers with long-duration liabilities. Investment in renewable energy infrastructure may provide a clever way to tackle that challenge.

The opportunity set in this arena is expansive. Last year, the world invested almost twice as much in clean energy as it did in fossil fuels, according to the International Energy Agency. Financing is increasingly coming from private capital providers who are able to structure assets that can meet life insurers’ unique needs.

These assets can come with investment-grade ratings from the major ratings agencies, which may increase relative attractiveness on a capital-adjusted yield and spread basis. And maturities in the 20-year range make them a good match for insurers with long-term liabilities.

Other potential benefits include:

  • High Return Potential: Assets typically sit on the lowest rung of the investment-grade ratings ladder, which usually means larger spreads and enhanced return potential over similar quality long-term assets, including utility bonds and publicly traded government and corporate debt. Illiquidity premiums also enhance return potential.
  • Diversification Benefits: Because these assets tend to have relatively low correlations with similar duration assets, they may offer an effective way to diversify insurers' portfolios.
  • Capital Efficiency: Certain renewable energy infrastructure — particularly in Europe — may warrant a substantial reduction in capital requirements.
  • Muted Default History: Defaults on private infrastructure financing have been consistently lower than those on comparable public corporate bonds issued by nonfinancial companies (Display).

Private Infrastructure Finance: A Strong Foundation

Traditionally, banks have provided a healthy share of the financing for renewable energy infrastructure. But projects have grown larger as bank balance sheets have become more constrained, with private credit assuming a larger role.

That’s good news for insurers because private lenders can pool projects into investment portfolios that may help reduce single-asset risk. For example, a pooled portfolio might include community solar projects across multiple states, all contracted with utilities or other investment-grade offtakers.

Private credit’s flexibility to structure assets in ways that meet insurers’ longer time horizons has also created a steadier source of capital for developers, who would otherwise be forced to rely on shorter-term bank financing.

US Offers a Broader Opportunity Set

Today, we see the most attractive opportunities in the US — for both US and European insurers. Clean energy is the fastest growing — and least expensive — electricity source in the country, and the market’s sheer size opens up broader investment options.

Renewable power is even more entrenched in Europe’s energy ecosystem, with solar and wind having overtaken fossil fuels, according to Ember, a think tank. But for now, few assets come with similarly insurance-friendly structures and investment-grade credit ratings.

The Tale of Tariffs: Assessing the Risks

US tariffs on a range of imports will affect energy infrastructure opportunities, particularly for projects being planned and waiting on imported equipment. But while China remains the largest single producer of solar panels, the widening of supply chains beyond China in recent years has blunted the impact of tariffs on new construction.

More importantly, the low cost of solar energy in recent years has prompted developers to fast-track projects, creating a healthy inventory of operating plants not subject to tariffs. These are the projects that fit most comfortably into insurance portfolios. They come with investment-grade ratings and can be structured as long-term debt that helps insurers match assets to liabilities.

Overall, we expect little effect on projects expected to come online in 2025, since equipment should already be in the country. Delays may pick up in 2026 from supply chain challenges. Potential changes to the Inflation Reduction Act (IRA) that reduce or eliminate tax credits may also slow momentum.

But we see these as short-term hiccups. Global energy demand is surging. In 2024, it rose by 2.2%, according to the IEA, a significant jump from the 1.3% annual average between 2013 and 2023. This adds up to trillions of dollars of investment per year on an annualized basis. Meeting that demand will require a healthy dose of renewable energy infrastructure, in our view.

Weighing the Regulatory Considerations

When assessing the opportunity, non-US insurers will have to consider the higher cost of currency hedges when matching US-dollar assets against non-dollar liabilities. This will require reviewing the costs of FX forwards and over-the-counter cross-country hedges and how currency volatility will affect those costs.

Regulatory considerations will vary across regions, too. In Europe, insurers should carefully review the rating methodology used — whether internal or external. Also important: determining whether the underlying asset can be classed as “qualifying infrastructure,” which can reduce capital requirements. Insurers that apply the matching adjustment mechanism will need to review any cash flow prepayment features to ensure compliance with all Matching Adjustment eligibility requirements.

For US-based insurers, most of the opportunity set is rated BBB- and carries a NAIC rating of 2.C, and may be considered as a component of private placement allocations.

Overall, though, we think these assets offer insurers with long liabilities an appealing opportunity to enhance returns on regulatory capital and diversification while reducing the risk of default. Tariffs have little impact on operating portfolios that are a good fit for insurance capital, and demand for power remains strong. We don’t expect that to change.

data.title: Carillon Chartwell Real Income Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

The economy is slowing

Cracks have started to form in what was recently thought to be the most resilient economy in the world. While the hard data points, such as gross domestic product (GDP) and payroll numbers, have remained resilient, they are backward-looking. The soft data points (i.e., survey results), have weakened significantly, particularly around the consumer, who comprises 70% of the U.S. economy. Uncertainty around tariffs and the job market are leading contributors to the heightened anxiety around the economy’s outlook. Though personal income continues to rise at an impressive rate, credit card and auto loan delinquencies are rising as well, bringing into question how much margin the consumer would have to soften the effects of any downward turn in the economy. In this regard, personal spending is already showing signs of a slowdown. We believe the degree to which the economy slows could therefore be a product of how long uncertainty remains around tariffs and fiscal spending.

Inflation and immigration

Much attention has deservedly been given to the effects of tariffs on inflation, but we feel an equally important topic is the new administration’s actions around immigration. Historically, immigration to the United States has averaged around 1 million people a year, but in 2023 that number swelled to 3 million, and in 2024 it remained elevated at 2.8 million. A large driver of the recent influx was a rapid increase in what some economists call “irregular immigrants” – that is, those who have either entered the United States as humanitarian immigrants or unlawfully, along with those who have overstayed their visas. Barclays’ economists estimate that these irregular immigrants have added 5.2 million workers to the labor force since 2021, which has helped drive down labor costs in construction, agriculture, food manufacturing, and hospitality. With irregular immigration effectively shut down, many work visas now revoked, and deportations ticking up, we are left to wonder what effect this could have on labor costs and long-run inflation.

Interest rates

It may be hard to believe, but interest rates, as measured by the yield on the 10-year U.S. Treasury note, have been range-bound for roughly two years; oscillating between 3.60% and 4.75% for most of this period. While inflation remains above the U.S. Federal Reserve’s target, growth has started to cool, which has kept a lid on the back end of the yield curve. We believe the secular bull market in bonds ended in 2021, and rates should eventually resume their trend higher in tandem with higher levels of inflation, but we see no reason for the recent range to break in the coming quarters given the growth headwinds the economy faces.

Credit spreads

Corporate credit spreads moved wider during the quarter. While the move in March was particularly pronounced, spreads are coming off historically tight levels and remain well below their 10-year average. Demand for new issuance remains resilient and the high-yield market tallied its busiest March since 2021, while the high-grade market produced nearly $600 billion of new paper, the highest amount for any first quarter on record. With growing interest in private credit, we think the most likely place to see any red flags in the credit market could be within leveraged loans. Many of the lower-quality credits that previously issued paper in the high-yield market have pivoted to the private credit markets, reducing the average credit quality in the leveraged loan market. While the returns in leveraged loans turned down in March, the move was rather muted. We will continue to monitor for any further deterioration.

Portfolio holdings are not stagnant and may change over time without prior notice. Past performance does not guarantee future results.
